data.title: Check Point: Market Prices Stagnation, But Partial Transition Can Drive Upside
data.description:
Introduction—The Architecture Shift

Check Point Software (CHKP) has a long track record of proactive breach protection. Enterprise customers trust its deterministic approach built over 3 decades of existence. Its firewalls and gateway-based security architecture are standards in the network security industry.

CHKP’s core architecture uses the concept of a gateway. In this dimension, traffic flows through network gateways where stateful inspection enforces policy. Intrusion prevention systems (IPS) and signature-based antivirus detect and block known threats. Originally delivered as Firewall-1, this functionality evolved into CHKP’s Software Blade architecture. Customers trusted this because of its deep packet inspection and broadly high-confidence blocking of known threats before they could execute.

To understand where Checkpoint fell behind, network gateways have lost their primacy as the primary control point. Today, they are just one layer among many. Applications are now delivered via SaaS over HTTPS and are hardly distinguishable from normal web traffic. Many users do not sit behind enterprise firewalls anymore - they operate from unmanaged devices across residential networks. North-south traffic’s primacy has been replaced by a mix of east-west traffic consisting of service-to-service communication via APIs, containers, and microservices. Some of these packets do not traverse a traditional firewall at all.

The concept of security itself has shifted. CrowdStrike’s Falcon Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) platform now operates directly at the endpoint - not the choke point. The concept is now behavioral analytics, not signatures. Threat detection is now probabilistic, not deterministic. There are cloud-native platforms like Zscaler (ZS) that route traffic through distributed proxies. These platforms rely on a zero trust policy based on identity and context, not network location. Even attack modes have shifted, and the concept of a single-perimeter security has lost primacy.

Check Point has been late in identifying the shift. That is what the company is navigating now.

Today’s Security Approach is Different

Within the Software Blade architecture, traffic was matched against known signatures and policy rules. There was a clear, deterministic logic that was used to block/allow traffic. Three things made this work: traffic was predictable, there were centralized enforcement points/choke points, and threats came with a signature and could be classified.

All three conditions have now weakened. First, we still have TLS inspection. However, with increasing HTTPS encryption, enterprise traffic is less visible now. Second, traffic doesn’t primarily flow through choke points anymore. Especially in cloud-native environments, much of the traffic now flows within Kubernetes clusters, serverless functions, and API gateways. Third, threats are no longer easily classifiable. They are often fileless, use legitimate tools like PowerShell or WMI, use dynamic AI-enabled adaptations, and so on. You cannot easily detect them using a signature-based approach.

Enforcement points have, therefore, shifted. They are now redistributed across distributed edges, SSE/ZTNA layers, and SASE architectures. At the same time, the threat model is now behavioral. Modern attacks do not rely on static payloads, using legitimate tools like PowerShell, WMI, and SSH; operate without files; move using stolen credentials; and AI-generated variants even adapt in real-time. These cannot be reliably stopped using signatures and predefined rules.

In such a fundamental shift, the gateway is just one of many signals - and often not the most important one.

Investment Framing—Where the Market is Mispricing CHKP

Once we agree that Check Point’s legacy architecture is outdated, the next question becomes, can they change? And the more important question—can the market rightly price their ability to change?

Currently, the market values CHKP as a mature, low-growth network security vendor. The market sees ~5-6% revenue growth, a decent 30-40% operating margin, and significant cash generation—and the market thinks “no re-acceleration potential.”

The market could be wrong. First, CHKP is not starting from scratch. If it reinvents itself, it doesn’t need to go all the way in order to be rerated. The transition is not trivial. They need to expand their architecture beyond only network enforcement. That would involve endpoint telemetry, identity-aware access, and cloud-native inspection. This will take multiple years of go-to-market and R&D shift. However, my point is that even a partial execution built on existing competency is enough to change the revenue profile.

Second, there are already subtle signs that this is already happening. Some segments, like the subscription business, are growing at a higher pace than the rest. In Q4, it grew 11% YoY to $325 million. That shows that CHKP is focusing on software-led recurring revenue models. Thirdly, CHKP is also investing in adjacent layers. In their Q4 earnings call, the company noted that they now secure their customer’s AI-enabled business “through 4 strategic solution pillars, each one of them a platform of its own. Hybrid Mesh Network Security, securing the infrastructure; Workspace Security, securing the employees; Exposure Management that provides situational awareness; and then finally, AI Security across all of these pillars.”

Given the multiples it is applying, however, the market is not yet appearing to see any major success in that transition. The market probably thinks expansion will happen within network security only, with only modest growth in adjacent layers and no real change in overall growth trajectory.

This creates an interesting angle. If CHKP remains primarily a network enforcement layer, the current valuation is fine. There’s stable growth, high margins, and strong free cash flow—but no premium valuation.

If the company is able to transition properly, however, their financial outlook will improve sharply. Companies that have modernized their architecture now get higher data ownership and greater pricing power. Endpoint and SASE are driving R&D spending in the network security business, and even a modest expansion in these can improve revenue; however, nothing of this sort is being priced in by the market.

Where CHKP Can Still Win

It is not necessary for Check Point to displace cloud-native leaders like CrowdStrike or Zscaler to succeed. Its one key advantage is its installed base of enterprise firewall customers. Here, they already control enforcement points and policy. They only need to build competence in adjacent layers—bundling cloud security, SASE, and endpoint capabilities into existing deployments. They would need to spend less on customer acquisition costs because they already have customers. They would only need incremental adoptions within existing contracts. There is no need to compete head-on with more advanced players. There are at least two hardware/tech areas where it is possible to expand quickly.

First, one segment for potential expansion is in hybrid networks plus cloud enforcement. CHKP already generates ~$2.7B revenue, growing at ~5-6%, largely from network-led deployments. If they can attain even a little bit of share in the large base of cloud security, that can move the needle. Let’s say they get an additional 3-4% incremental growth from expanding into cloud and hybrid deployments; then the total revenue growth gets into high single digits (~8-10%)—that doesn’t need a huge breakthrough anywhere else.

Second, SASE could be a good place to look into. The global SASE market is growing faster than CHKP’s core business. It doesn’t need to become a leader here; just basic penetration within its installed base is all that’s needed for now. For context, Zscaler has 3,886 customers generating over $100,000 in ARR—so we may say that enterprise SASE deployments typically scale into six-figure annual contracts. CHKP has 100,000 global customers. If even 5–10% of this base adopts SASE over time—equivalent to roughly 5,000–10,000 customers—at a conservative $50,000–$100,000 per deployment annually, this translates to approximately $250M–$1B in incremental revenue. This is material for growth but still does not imply category leadership. Consolidated growth could rise to ~8-13% under such a scenario.

To get there, the math is straightforward:

To get to this growth, there is no need to assume category leadership in any one segment. The key idea is that Check Point does not need to be a leader—just meaningful participation in these growth markets is enough to change its revenue profile as well as its market perception.

Valuation—What is Priced vs. What Changes

To anchor all that technology and theory in hard numbers, let’s start with the current baseline. Currently, CHKP trades at roughly ~13-14x forward earnings (non-GAAP) versus a sector median of ~21x. That is a nearly 35% discount coming from its currently slow, mature 5-6% growth.

Let’s take the base case—the one that’s currently priced in. We start with revenue of ~$2.7B, a multi-year EPS approximation of ~$11.5, we set growth at ~5-6%, and we use a ~13-14x price multiple.

When we project 3-year EPS growth at ~7% CAGR, we get ~$14. Applying the same multiple, we get $196. With some modest re-rating and some buybacks, we can get to $200-$230. This gives us a low-volatility, modest compounding outcome. This is our base case.

Now let’s say partial transition works, revenue growth becomes ~10-12% CAGR, and some decent operating leverage and mix creates a ~13-15% CAGR EPS growth. So at year 3, taking 14% as the midpoint, we get EPS of $17.04.

Now comes the key variable—multiple. We don’t need to assume parity with the sector (~20–25x). Even partial success could expand the multiple to ~16–18x. At 16x, we get ~$272, and at 18x, we get $307 as the valuation. So our bull case range is ~$270-310. This assumption is consistent with how the market values transitioning infrastructure vendors—companies like Cisco, IBM, and Oracle, which continue to generate strong cash flows but trade in a ~18–22x range due to slower growth and legacy exposure. Even in a successful transition, Check Point is more likely to move toward the upper end of that range rather than re-rate to platform-level multiples.

This is an achievable target because we only need an additional 3-4% from SASE/cloud, maybe 2-3% more from hybrid expansion, and 2-3% from subscription mix. That easily doubles the growth without needing any unrealistic assumption like category leadership.

While we are at it, let’s also mathematicize the bear case. This occurs due to structural erosion—not just a failure to achieve relevancy. Assume the downside is not just flat, but it is negative operating leverage. So revenue growth becomes ~0-3%, and EPS growth is ~0% or even slightly negative due to pricing pressure and other structural issues. Now, EPS at year 3 goes down to ~$10–11. Apply a compressed multiple of ~11-12x for what is now essentially no-growth software.

Then the valuation becomes ~$110 at the lower end of that range and ~$132 at the higher end.

What will cause such a scenario? A number of technology missteps - say firewalls increasingly become bypassed, SASE vendors capture traffic directly instead of going through a company like CHKP, or CHKP fails to attach new products. Here we have a slow redundancy scenario, not a complete collapse—just what the bear case says.

To put all that in a table:

Scenario

Value

This exercise shows us something very important—and positive. The downside does not require a collapse. It can emerge gradually if the gateway becomes obsolete faster than Check Point can replace it with new control layers. That deterioration in renewal rates can directly pressure both revenue and margins. However, even here, the downside is limited to roughly ~10–25%, because the business is supported by the company’s installed base, mission-critical positioning, and high-margin recurring revenue.

On the other hand, the upside can approach 2x over time, even if driven by just a partial success in adjacent layers like SASE and cloud security.

That asymmetry makes CHKP attractive at current levels.

data.title: Devon Energy: Merger Deal, Iran War, Price Tailwinds
data.description:
Devon Energy (DVN) is going to be a major beneficiary, like so many other oil and gas companies, from surging petroleum prices following America's and Israel's attacks on Iranian military installations, starting at the end of February. Although petroleum prices recently dropped on hopes of a peace agreement, U.S. President Trump recently threatened an escalating pace of military strikes on Iran again. As the risk of a prolonged military campaign in the Middle East is looming larger, I see U.S.-focused independent exploration and production companies like Devon Energy as strong investment choices for investors.

More importantly, Devon Energy just a short while ago announced a transformative merger with Coterra Energy, which will allow the energy enterprise to grow its production footprint, diversify more into natural gas, and potentially capture significant merger synergies.

Previous Rating

I rated independent oil and gas company Devon Energy a strong buy in June 2025 -- A Capital Return Play -- following the U.S.'s strike on Iran's nuclear facilities, mainly because of a strong price reaction at the time in petroleum markets. Most importantly, the recent escalation between the U.S. and Iran, which spread the conflict to other Gulf countries and led Iran to block the vitally important Strait of Hormuz, creates significant earnings upside for Devon Energy. I believe shares of Devon Energy are too cheap and have long-term upside revaluation potential, whether or not the U.S. will end its war with Iran in the short term.

Iran War Backdrop Creates Earnings Upside for Devon Energy

Devon Energy is headed for a strong first-quarter earnings report given that petroleum prices have surged to more than $100 per barrel following America's and Israel's combined attack on Iran. The increase in petroleum prices since February 28, 2026, is set to create a major profit windfall for integrated as well as independent exploration and production companies like Devon Energy that are heavily focused on oil production.

Most importantly, the energy enterprise's assets are all located in the U.S., and not in the Middle East, which safeguards the firm's production at a time of increasing supply uncertainty. In the fourth quarter, Devon Energy averaged 390 MBbl/d in oil production vs. the same production volume in the preceding quarter. Including natural gas and NGL production, Devon Energy had an oil-equivalent production of 851 MBOED in the fourth quarter, which continues to come mainly from the core Delaware Basin (~61%).

With the Coterra Energy acquisition (discussed below), however, Devon Energy's total production volume is set to grow at a stronger rate going forward, mainly because it is going to add faster-growing non-oil assets to its production mix.

Merger Deal With Coterra Energy Set to Expand Devon Energy's Operational Footprint

On February 2, 2026, Devon Energy announced a transformative all-stock merger with Coterra Energy, a major natural gas shale company in the U.S., which valued the enterprise at $58.0B, based off of enterprise value. The transaction will allow Devon Energy to combine its oil-heavy Permian assets with Coterra's high-quality Marcellus gas assets, creating a more diversified energy enterprise in the process with cash flow upside related to growing operational scale.

The combined entity is set to have a pro forma production volume of 1.6M barrels of oil equivalent per day, with approximately 48% coming from Coterra Energy. Most importantly, Devon Energy said in its merger announcement that it expects the transaction to be accretive to free cash flow and that it targets $1.0B in annualized cost savings by FY 2027.

Devon Energy's Valuation

Devon Energy has seen a significant repricing to the upside after the outbreak of hostilities in the Middle East at the end of February. Although shares have slightly corrected to the downside at the end of March in hopes of a peace agreement between the U.S. and Iran, signs are pointing to a continued escalation in the military conflict.

Shares of Devon Energy are currently valued at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 12.2X, which places Devon Energy's shares above the 3-year average price-to-earnings ratio of 10.2X. However, I would hardly say that the energy enterprise is overvalued here, especially not in the context of other energy companies with an upstream business focus.

ConocoPhillips (COP) and Diamondback Energy (FANG) are trading at forward P/E ratios of 18.4X and 13.4X, while EOG Resources (EOG) is priced at 11.6X forward earnings. The industry group average P/E ratio here is 13.9X, which would leave room for Devon Energy to revalue a little further to the upside.

Assuming that the Middle East conflict between the U.S., Israel, and Iran drags on for longer (6 months or more), I would expect that petroleum prices remain elevated, potentially above $100 per barrel. This obviously poses a serious earnings and free cash flow growth catalyst for Devon Energy, which achieves approximately 46% of production from its oil wells.

I believe that Devon Energy could trade at a ~15.0X P/E ratio easily, assuming that the transaction with Coterra Energy closes as expected in the second quarter and merger synergies can be realized. A 15.0X earnings multiplier, applied to a consensus FY 2027 earnings estimate of $4.13 per share, calculates to a fair value of $62 per share... which is about 25% above the firm's current market price.

Risks With Devon Energy

The biggest risk for Devon Energy in the short term is obviously a normalization of the price picture in the crude oil market. A peace agreement between the United States and Iran would likely bring down energy prices a lot, potentially back to pre-war price levels of $70 per barrel. This would almost certainly eradicate Devon Energy's potential for a major profit windfall and lead to a valuation correction. What would change my mind about Devon Energy specifically is if the company failed to close the merger with Coterra Energy or if its merger synergies could not be realized.

Final Thoughts

Devon Energy, as an independent oil and gas exploration company, is a primary beneficiary of rising oil prices. While shares of Devon Energy have repriced higher following the outbreak of a new war in the Middle East in February, the energy enterprise is still trading at a very affordable P/E ratio, most certainly when compared against ConocoPhillips, which is also aggressively growing its production base in the Permian. Devon Energy is now a play on Coterra-related merger benefits and an expansion of its production footprint in the long term, but also higher price realizations in the short term. I like the cheap valuation of Devon Energy's shares and would continue to recommend DVN for energy-oriented growth investors, despite a 42% price gain since my last recommendation.

data.title: S&P 500: Prepare For Change (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Last week's action in the S&P500 (SPY) was again dictated by war-related news stories, and the familiar early week recovery rally happened to be the same size as the early week recovery rallies (in futures) on March 9th and March 23rd. However, the usual mid-week reversal and drop on Thursday recovered, and the weekly close was a notable change. This article looks at how and why the technicals have shifted positive, and what this means for the coming weeks.

S&P500 Monthly

Last Tuesday's rally meant that the March bar did not close dead on the lows, which dulled the immediate downside bias. That said, the bounce was not large enough to save the bar from a bearish pattern and it looks very much like March 2025. The Q1 bar on the quarterly chart also closed with a bearish pattern.

Another doubt stems from the lack of good support at the 6316 low. The 20SMA at 6280ish was not reached, while the major level of 6147 is yet to be tested. Of course, there's nothing to say that major levels have to be touched, but reversing in no man's land is not ideal for a sustainable bottom.

April is typically a bullish month - in fact, it ranks second for performance with an average gain of 1.4% - but that doesn't mean it can't drop first (like last year), or just trade inside the large March range to set up a drop in May.

Resistance is 6775 and 7002.

A DeMARK exhaustion count completed in February and is having an effect.

S&P500 Weekly

The weekly chart underwent a notable change last week as it finally closed higher after four straight weeks of closes at new 2026 lows. This came right on cue as a weekly downside Demark exhaustion signal completed on bar 9 (of 9).

This signal is quite rare as it takes 9 down weeks and SPX corrections are usually much shorter. There was a signal at the 2025 low, which bottomed on bar 8, then at the very low of the 2022 bear market, again on bar 8. Before that, Jan '16 on bar 8, June '12 on bar 9, and March '09 (the crash bottom) on bar 8.

The last time this signal completed and failed to lead to a sustainable bottom was way back in October '08. I suppose that's a comfort but also a warning: if this signal fails, watch out below.

The 6316 low didn't really come at any compelling weekly support, but the rally reclaimed the 50SMA and the 6520-50 lows of the range.

Resistance if 6764 and the 20SMA, which will dip into the same area. 6850ish will also be resistance at the high volume (distribution) level.

S&P500 Daily

Monday's dip tested minor resistance pointed out in last weekend's article at 6343. However, there wasn't any immediate sign of a bullish reaction as the session closed weak, and it was only when Tuesday's session held the opening lows and rallied through the gap at 6453 that we could be more certain of a recovery. Tuesday's bullish session was followed by Wednesday's gap higher out of the channel and Thursday's re-test and higher close. It was the first green Thursday in ten weeks.

Just to muddy the waters slightly, the futures channel, which I consider more important as much of the action has taken place out of hours, did not convincingly break. However, I suspect it breaks next week and the intraday charts have a potential inverse head and shoulders pattern that would trigger on a move above 6609.

Support is 6480 and 6385.

Resistance is at the 200SMA and 50% retrace in the 6640-50 area. After that, 6764 is a major level.

An upside Demark exhaustion signal will be on bar 3 (of 9) on Monday so no reaction is expected next week.

Drivers/Events

The fog of war has not lifted. Trump threatened to bomb Iran into the Stone Age in a TV address on Wednesday, and despite some positive hints from Iran over the course of the week, they have rejected negotiations.

Who was buying into a long weekend when an escalation looked certain, and why?

Whoever it was must have been confident.

On the positive side, there has finally been some increased passage through the Strait. Also, Trump has handed over responsibility for its re-opening and seems prepared to end the war in 2-3 weeks, whether it is open or not. What that means for oil prices remains to be seen, but at least there is an easy "out" for the US.

Friday's jobs report was strong with 178k jobs added, while the unemployment rate dropped to 4.3%. That's probably a bit stronger than the markets would have liked as further rate cuts hinge on a cooling labour market. As Fed Chair Powell said on Monday,

There's sort of downside risk ​to the labor market, which suggests keep rates low, but there's upside risk to inflation, which suggests maybe don't keep rates low.

Remove the downside risk to the labour market, and there is no justification for cuts. I don't believe they will hike, but it is something markets may fret over.

Next week's data highlight will be CPI on Friday. This will certainly jump higher due to the oil price shock, but by how much? Expectations are for the MoM to rise from 0.3% (already high) to 1.0%, and for the YoY to move from 2.4% to 3.4%. EU figures were released last week, and annual inflation rose from 1.9% to 2.5%. The problem is they will likely rise further in the coming months.

Probable Moves

Last week brought some notable technical changes as the Demark exhaustion signal had a clear effect, the cash channel broke, and the weekly chart closed strong. I can't tell what the newsflow will be like ahead of Monday's open, but the market's reaction to any bad news is likely to be less bearish, and I expect higher lows to hold. Indeed, Thursday's overnight low of 6463 has a decent chance of holding.

While a larger recovery should be underway, there are reasons to believe it will fade and set up another leg down. The quarterly/monthly charts don't look like they have bottomed, and the decline did not even reach the 38% Fibonacci retrace of the 2025-2026 bull market. 50-61% remains my expectation.

The 200SMA around 6650 could act as initial resistance next week, but 6764 is the likely destination for this bounce. 6850 is possible, but not as probable.

When this recovery concludes, 6150ish should be the next downside target.

data.title: Wall Street Brunch: Trump Ramps Up Iran Threats
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Trump threatens big Tuesday Iran strikes over Strait deadline. (0:17) Markets brace for hotter inflation. (1:50) Payrolls crush expectations. (2:51)

The following is an abridged transcript:

President Donald Trump intensified his rhetoric toward Iran, threatening major infrastructure strikes if the Strait of Hormuz is not reopened under a 48-hour deadline he set Saturday morning.

On Sunday, Trump posted: “Tuesday will be Power Plant Day, and Bridge Day, all wrapped up in one, in Iran. There will be nothing like it!!! Open the F-----n’ Strait, you crazy bastards, or you’ll be living in Hell - JUST WATCH! Praise be to Allah.”

The not-safe-for-work message — notable for its tone, explicit language, and invocation of Allah on Easter — triggered an immediate uproar across social media.

The post followed one a day earlier in which Trump warned that “all hell” would rain down if the Strait was not reopened.

Later Sunday, in a phone interview with Fox News correspondent Trey Yingst, Trump said negotiations with Iranian officials remain active. But if “they don’t make a deal and fast,” he is “considering blowing everything up and taking over the oil,” according to Yingst’s account.

Prediction markets showed limited movement following the comments.

On Kalshi, the odds of traffic in the Strait returning to normal by May 15 held around 14%, roughly unchanged. Odds of reopening by June 1 rose to 30%, up 7 percentage points.

On Polymarket, the probability of the conflict ending by May 15 ticked up to 28% from 25%, while odds of resolution by June 30 slipped to 55% from 60%.

Meanwhile, OPEC+ members are preparing to approve a modest increase in May oil (USO) (BNO) production targets despite the disruption to flows.

Bloomberg reports that key producers, including Saudi Arabia and Russia, are expected to back a plan to raise quotas by roughly 206K bpd during a virtual meeting.

On the economic front, this week is all about inflation.

On Thursday, the Fed’s preferred inflation gauge — core PCE — arrives alongside consumption data. But February’s numbers may already feel dated given the recent energy surge. Core PCE is expected to rise 0.4% M/M, with the annual rate edging down to 3%.

Friday’s March CPI report will be more closely watched for the early impact of higher oil prices.

Headline CPI is forecast to jump 1% M/M, pushing the annual rate up to 3.4% from 2.4%. Core CPI, which excludes food and energy, is seen rising 0.3% on the month, with the annual rate ticking up to 2.7% from 2.5%.

While core CPI excludes energy directly, higher oil prices can filter through other components — such as airfare — as airlines adjust to rising jet fuel costs.

SA analyst Damir Tokic says if the war drags on, “inflation expectations are likely to de-anchor as inflation becomes more persistent, at which point the Fed will need to start hiking.”

Investors will also get their first full trading reaction to Friday’s March jobs report, released during the market holiday.

Nonfarm payrolls rose 178K, more than triple the 51K consensus estimate. The unemployment rate edged down to 4.3%.

Heather Long, chief economist at Navy Federal Credit Union, noted solid hiring across several industries, but warned that slowing wage growth could “hurt many workers.”

Delta Air Lines (DAL) headlines a thin calendar, with analysts expecting EPS of $0.60 on revenue of about $14.9B. The carrier raised its Q1 revenue outlook in March, citing healthy demand.

SA analyst Tim Dunn notes Delta’s Trainer refinery could save as much as $1B relative to peers amid elevated crack spreads and geopolitical risk.

Investors will also be listening for commentary on TSA staffing pressures during the government shutdown.

Also on the calendar:

Levi Strauss (LEVI) reports Tuesday. Constellation Brands (STZ) and Applied Digital (APLD) join Delta on Wednesday. BlackBerry (BB) weighs in Thursday.

And for income investors, JPMorgan Chase (JPM) goes ex-dividend Monday, with payment scheduled for April 30.

The Gap (GAP) goes ex-dividend Wednesday, paying April 29.

AT&T (T) and Verizon (VZ) go ex-dividend Friday. Both pay May 1.

data.title: Buy 7 S&P 500 April Dividend Dogs
data.description:
Foreword

Introducing his Kiplinger Investing newsletter article, Dan Burrows notes, “Sometimes stocks with the highest dividend yields can be fool's gold. And this could be pertinent to some of the stocks with the highest dividend yields in the S&P 500 today.

Be forewarned that in some cases, the names below have entered elite-yield territory only because their share prices have come under stress. In turn, that has some analysts concerned about the sustainability of payouts going forward.”

While most of this collection of S&P500 dividend stocks over-priced or reveals only skinny dividends, seven of the thirteen highest yield 'safer' S&P500 April Dogs are ready to buy. The list was screened to remove stocks showing negative annual returns this month.

April finds: VICI Properties (VICI); Verizon (VZ); Franklin Resources (BEN); Ford Motor (F); Host Hotels (HST); AT&T (T); KeyCorp (KEY), and Regions (RF) living up to the dogcatcher ideal of annual dividends from $1K invested exceeding single share prices. All seven are also 'safer' because they have free cash flow adequate to support their dividends.

With renewed downside market pressure of up to 20%, all ten of the top 'safer' dividend dogs could become ideal, fair-priced buys, with annual yield (from $1K invested) meeting or exceeding their single share prices.

[See a summary of the top ten fair-priced S&P500 Dogs in Actionable Conclusion 21 near the midpoint of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 25.96% To 44.73% Net Gains From Top-Ten S&P500 Dogs By April 2027

Four of the ten top dividend-yield S&P500 divi-dogs (tinted gray in the chart below) were also top ten price gainers based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this April, 2026 yield-based forecast for S&P500 dogs, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 40% accurate.

Estimated dividend returns from $1,000.00 invested in each of the ten highest-yielding S&P500 stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the estimates below. (Note: one-year target prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to April, 2027 were:

BXP Inc. (BXP) was projected to net $447.32, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 22 analysts, less broker fees. The beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 3% greater than the market as a whole.

Alexandria Real Estate (ARE) was projected to net $408.01, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 16 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this 33% greater than the market as a whole.

Amcor (AMCR) was projected to net $340.42 based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 13 analysts, less broker fees. The beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 29% less than the market as a whole.

VICI Properties was projected to net $323.18, based on the median of target price estimates from 24 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The beta number showed this 30% less than the market as a whole.

International Paper Co. (IP) was projected to net $317.76, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 13 analysts, less broker fees. The beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 14% over the market as a whole.

Skyworks Solutions, Inc. (SWKS) was projected to net $305.72, based on the median of target price estimates from 25 analysts, less broker fees. The beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 31% over the market as a whole.

Hormel Foods (HRL) was projected to net $294.49 based on the median of target price estimates from 10 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 64% under the market as a whole.

Invitation Homes Inc. (INVH) was projected to net $285.77, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 24 analysts, less broker fees. The beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 19% less than the market as a whole.

Healthpeak Properties (DOC) was projected to net $275.49, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 18 analysts, less broker fees. The beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 10% over the market as a whole.

Crown Castle Inc. (CCI) was projected to net $259.64 based on the median of target prices estimated by 21 analysts, less broker fees. The beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 5% less than the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 32.58% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten S&P500 dividend stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 5% over the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends; (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

18 S&P500 Dogs By Total Return Estimates

This reader-suggested addition to my monthly report ranks the top 18/50 S&P500 top yield stocks by total returns. As the reader commented, “Total returns are the critical screen for many income investors.” To this end, 32 negative returns were not highlighted.

50 S&P500 Dogs By Analyst Target-Gain Estimates

The April, 2026 S&P500 50 By Yield

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): April S&P500 Dividend Stocks By Yield Ranged From 6.25% To 8.91%

The Top ten April S&P500 Dogs represented five of eleven Morningstar sectors.

Five consumer staple stalwarts, placed first, third, fourth, seventh, and ninth: Conagra Brands (CAG) [1], Kraft Heinz (KHC) [3], Campbell's (CPB) [4], General Mills (GIS) [7], and Altria Group (MO) [9].

Meanwhile, two real estate stocks placed second, and sixth: Healthpeak Properties (DOC) [2], and VICI Properties [6].

Fifth place went to the lone industrials member, United Parcel Service (UPS) [5], and eighth place belonged to the materials representative, Amcor [8].

Finally, one technology member captured the tenth slot, HP Inc. (HPQ) [10], to complete the March top ten Dogs of the S&P500 by yield.

Dividend vs Price

The graphic above shows the relative strengths of the top ten S&P500 dogs by dividend and price as of market close 4/1/2026.

In April two of the top-ten S&P500 dogs by yield showed less than ideal status: United Parcel and Altria. The other eight projected annual free cash flow yields (from $1k invested) exceeding their single share prices.

S&P500 Costs per Dividend Dollar as of 4/1/26

The remaining bottom forty on the above chart shows more S&P500 dogs subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is calculated the cost per dividend dollar for each stock.

April’s least expensive dividend dog of the S&P500 is Conagra at $11.23 per dividend dollar, and the most expensive of the top 50 is Host Hotels (HST) at $23.95 per dividend dollar. Of all 500, however, Nvidia (NVDA) has the most expensive dividend at $4,587.16.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Fifteen of Fifty Top Yield S&P500 Dividend Dogs Show Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash to pay dividends. The April cash-poor fifteen are: United Parcel, Amcor, Pfizer, Kimberly-Clark, International Paper, Hormel Foods, Mid-America Apartment, AES Corp, Edison International, ONEOK, Simon Property, Eversource Energy, Camden Property, Dominion Energy, and Federal Realty. They are considered unsafe to buy due to dividend yields exceeding their free cash flow yields.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, you must look for opportune pullbacks in price to add to your position and improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pullback opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, wars, uncertainty, deals, trades, and reciprocity, too.

Price Drops or Dividend Increases Could Get All Top 10 S&P500 Dogs Back to "Fair Price" Rates For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust the share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, nine of the top ten are already ideally priced. However, United Parcel, is $17.11 outside the ideal zone.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) 21.12% To 40.34% Top Ten S&P500 Dividend Upsides To April 2027; (32), No Downsiders Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target estimates provided another tool to dig out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 7.00% Disadvantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 S&P500 Dogs To April, 2027

Ten-top S&P500 April-updated yield (dividend/price) results, as verified by YCharts, made the following ranking:

As noted above, top-ten S&P500 dogs selected 4/1/26 by yield, represented five of eleven sectors. (Communication services, consumer discretionary, energy, financials, health care, and utilities, missed the top-ten cut.)

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected The 5 Lowest-Priced of Ten Highest-Yield S&P500 Dogs (34) To Deliver 14.95% Vs. (35) 16.08% Net Gains by All Ten Come April 2027

$5,000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten S&P500 Dividend Kennel by yield were predicted by analysts 1-year targets to deliver 7% LESS gain than $5,000 invested in all ten. The eighth lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, Amcor, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 34.04%.

The five lowest-priced S&P500 top-yield dogs for April were: Conagra; Healthpeak; HP Inc; Kraft Heinz; Campbell's, with prices ranging from $15.52 to $22.38.

Five higher-priced S&P500 top-yield dogs as of April 1 were: VICI Properties; General Mills; Amcor; Altria; United Parcel, with prices ranging from $27.46 to $97.91.

Differentiating five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten utilizes Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market. Plainly, O'Higgins's method also applies to S&P 500 dividend dogs.

Caution is warranted, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As the feckless Fed and trade war-fueled slide and the AI bubble gain momentum into 2026, with the exception of a very few “Hot” and “Meme” stocks, watch most of the 50 top return S&P500 Dogs become fairly priced… slowly, surely, or quite suddenly.

Afterword

Buy Any of Seven IDEAL 'Safer' S&P500 April DiviDogs; Watch Many More

In late March 2026, 14 of the top 54 S&P500 Dividend Dogs with positive one-year returns were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed free-cash-flow yields greater than dividend yields. YCharts 4/1/26 data showed seven IDEAL specimens.

How S&P500 Safer Dividend Vs. Price Results Could Produce A "Fair Price” Results For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals that five dividends from $1K invested in these top ten 'safer' dividend S&P500 dogs conform to the dogcatcher standard of being equal to (or less than) the price of a single share. Those five are VICI Properties, Verizon, Franklin Resources, Ford, and Host, still standing after surviving the 'safer' dividend gauntlet.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount, and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal the single share price of each stock, a so-called “fair price.”

A market correction of just 24% is required to put all 10 “safer” survivors above or equal to their dividend payouts from $1k invested.

There are, of course, other ways for dividends from $1K invested to exceed the price of a single share of stock. That would be for the boards of directors of these companies to raise dividends or create stock splits to bring prices to the dogcatcher ideal level. That's a lot to ask with all the corporate funds doing so much “good” elsewhere.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher Dogs of the S&P500 updates, out here, in public, away from the paywalled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

Footnotes

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

The 7 IDEAL Dogs of the S&P500

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" Dow dog dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations.

Disclaimer: This article is for informational and educational purposes only and should not be construed to constitute investment advice. Nothing contained herein shall constitute a solicitation, recommendation, or endorsement to buy or sell any security. Prices and returns on equities in this article, except as noted, are listed without consideration of fees, commissions, taxes, penalties, or interest payable due to purchasing, holding, or selling the same.

Graphs and charts were compiled by Rydlun & Co., LLC from data derived from YCharts, finance.yahoo.com, and analyst mean target price by YCharts. Open-source dog art from dividenddogcatcher.com.

data.title: Genesis Energy: Cash Flow Growth Likely To Slow Down Going Forward (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Genesis Energy, L.P. (GEL) is a unique master limited partnership that primarily makes its money through the ownership and operation of a large network of offshore pipelines. While the offshore pipeline unit is by far the company’s largest source of income, it also owns and operates a fleet of barges and boats that transport resources through the inland waterways of the United States. Genesis Energy also has an onshore crude oil and terminals infrastructure network, like many of the other midstream master limited partnerships that we have discussed over the years, but this is only a relatively small proportion of the company’s operations. Immediately, we notice that this is a very different asset base than most midstream MLPs possess, and that could allow Genesis Energy to provide a certain amount of diversification to a portfolio. Genesis Energy also enjoys some near-term growth potential as some of the large offshore projects within its footprint come online over the remainder of this year. However, the company has a relatively low yield compared to many of the other master limited partnerships in the market, and that may limit its appeal among the more income-hungry investors out there. In addition, the company's growth over the next two years will probably not be nearly as great as what we saw in 2025.

About Genesis Energy

Genesis Energy is one of the only master limited partnerships that is still in the market that operates an offshore pipeline network. Prior to the COVID-19 pandemic, there were other midstream partnerships that operated in the Gulf of America, such as American Midstream Partners and BP Midstream Partners. These two companies have since both been merged into larger corporate entities and no longer trade independently. This leaves Genesis Energy as one of the only options available for investors who believe that the production of crude oil and natural gas in the Gulf of America.

Please note that for the purposes of this article, I will use the name Gulf of America to describe the body of water south of the continental United States. This name remains somewhat controversial, and this body of water is known as the Gulf of Mexico internationally. Genesis Energy refers to the body of water as the Gulf of America in its own financial reports and investor materials, so that is the name that I have opted to use in this article. I am not taking a political position with respect to the naming controversy; I am just using it for consistency with the company’s own documentation.

The company’s offshore operations in the Gulf are by far Genesis Energy’s largest source of revenue and cash flow. For the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2025, approximately 67% of Genesis Energy’s reported segment margin came from the Gulf of America pipeline operations. This is substantially more than came from any of the company’s other two business units:

Business Unit

% of Segment Margin – LTM

Offshore Pipeline Transportation

67%

Marine Transportation

20%

Onshore Transportation & Services

13%

Obviously, this is a very different earnings and cash flow profile than that possessed by most midstream companies. The well-known midstream partnerships such as Energy Transfer (ET), MPLX (MPLX), Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), Plains All American Pipeline (PAA), and Western Midstream Partners (WES) do not have any offshore pipelines at all. While Kinder Morgan (KMI) does have a marine transportation unit, it is such a small proportion of the company’s overall cash flows that investors and analysts frequently overlook or ignore it. This is not the case with Genesis Energy, as all three of these business units represent a significant enough proportion of the company’s profits that we should not ignore any of them.

Genesis Energy’s offshore infrastructure consists of approximately 2,300 miles of pipelines that are primarily centered around the central portion of the Gulf of America:

There are a few pipeline systems located in the area that Genesis Energy has a partial or complete ownership of. Here they are:

Pipeline System

Genesis Ownership

Poseidon Oil Pipeline

64%

Cameron Highway Oil Pipeline System

64%

Eugene Island Pipeline System

29%

Odyssey Pipeline

29%

Nautilus System

25.7%

High Island Offshore System

100%

* This chart does not include the pipeline laterals that simply pick up the oil or natural gas from an oil platform and transport it to one of the larger systems shown in the chart. Genesis Energy owns 100% of most of these laterals, which can be seen by clicking on the link to the company’s website.

As we can see, the only one of the systems in which Genesis Energy has a full ownership position is the High Island Offshore System. All of the remaining assets have the company sharing ownership with someone else, and in three situations, the company does not even have majority control. This is not exactly unusual in the midstream space, as many large projects are undertaken by a consortium of companies rather than a single one. The biggest reason for this is that the capital costs of constructing pipelines or other infrastructure can be immense. As companies all face capital constraints, it makes more sense to work together to pool capital and share ownership of multiple pipelines rather than only investing in one and tying up a significant portion of the capital that is available to the company. Upstream oil companies also tend to share costs and ownership of larger projects for the same reason – it helps to diversify risk. We do this as investors, too. Rather than putting all of our money into a single stock, we split our money up and take smaller positions in multiple stocks and other asset classes. It greatly reduces our risks overall, and that is the same concept at play here. Genesis Energy receives its proportional portion of the cash flows from each of the pipelines in which it holds an equity stake, so the company still benefits from volume growth through any of the pipelines even if it does not have 100% ownership.

This is a good thing because there has been growth in offshore production in the Gulf of America, despite the bad reputation that it got following the Macondo disaster (which also prompted the Federal government to reduce the issuance of new offshore drilling permits). In fact, over the period from 2012 through the end of 2025, the volume of hydrocarbons moving through the Poseidon Oil Pipeline system and the Cameron Highway Oil Pipeline System increased by 131%:

Offshore pipelines have a very similar business model to the onshore pipeline systems that are operated by companies such as Energy Transfer and Enterprise Products Partners. In short, the pipeline owner gets paid a fee that is based on the volume of hydrocarbon products moving through the pipeline. Thus, whenever there is an increase in volumes, as we have seen since 2012, the revenue and cash flow generated by the pipeline increase.

With that said, Genesis Energy has not seen its operating cash flow increase in recent years. As we can see here, the company’s full-year 2025 operating cash flow actually came in lower than in 2017:

(all figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

However, we need to keep in mind that these figures are for the company as a whole and not just the offshore pipeline unit. Early last year, Genesis Energy sold its soda ash manufacturing business to WE Soda for a price of $1.425 billion. That business was previously one of the company’s largest business units. For example, back in 2024, the company’s Soda & Sulfur Services business accounted for 28% of Genesis Energy’s segment margin. Obviously, following the sale of this business unit, Genesis Energy stopped receiving that unit’s contribution to its cash flow. This alone could offset the growth that the offshore pipelines experienced.

We actually do see this if we look at the company’s most recent results. For the three-month period that ended on December 31, 2017, Genesis Energy’s offshore pipeline transportation unit reported a segment margin of $74.012 million. For the three-month period that ended on December 31, 2025, the same unit had a segment margin of $120.209 million. Thus, the company’s pipeline unit did indeed post growth despite the lack of operating cash flow growth.

In fact, Genesis Energy’s offshore pipeline unit delivered very strong growth in 2025 alone. For the three-month period that ended on December 31, 2024, the company’s offshore pipeline unit had a segment margin of $76.700 million. That means that the offshore pipeline transportation unit delivered a year-over-year growth rate of 56.73%. This very strong year-over-year growth rate should not be a surprise to Energy Profits in Dividends subscribers. There were a few large upstream projects in the Gulf of America located in the footprint of the company’s infrastructure that were expected to come online in 2025, and subscribers to the service know that I expected 2025 to be something of a transformational year for the company (and suggested purchasing it multiple times prior to 2025). We can clearly see in Genesis Energy’s fourth-quarter 2025 earnings results that this was the case. Subscribers to the service have also realized fairly handsome performance from this company as Genesis Energy managed to outperform the S&P 500 Index over the past five years:

Thus, this growth was not much of a surprise to those who have been watching this company over the years since the pandemic.

Unfortunately, though, it appears as though the strongest growth may be behind us. Over the period from 2015 to the present, there were 48 oil fields that started producing in the Gulf of America. This drove the region’s production from approximately 1.515 million barrels of oil per day to roughly 1.9 million barrels per day over that period. With the projects that came online in 2025, such as Salamanca (which started producing in September), the Gulf of America should see average daily production of around 1.980 million barrels of oil per day. That level could be the peak production for the near-term, though, as it is unclear whether or not there will be enough new production coming online in 2027 to offset the natural decline rates of the older fields in the Gulf. The U.S. Energy Information Administration predicts that production in the Gulf of America will decline in 2027, putting the average production level at about 1.870 million barrels per day:

Admittedly, the U.S. Energy Information Administration does believe that onshore production (such as tight oil plays) will make up for the difference and cause the nation’s production as a whole to rise in 2027. However, Genesis Energy does not really benefit from a surge in onshore production.

With that said, there are still a few projects under development in the Gulf of America that are located within relatively close proximity to Genesis Energy’s infrastructure. One of the most important of these is the Monument project that is currently being developed by Beacon Offshore. This project is located about 27 kilometers (17 miles) from the Shenandoah project that started producing last year, and which is already utilizing Genesis Energy’s offshore infrastructure to transport its production back to the shore. The Monument project is using a tieback with Shenandoah, which basically means that the production at Monument will be transported right back to Shenandoah. From there, the hydrocarbons produced by Monument will ultimately be transported back to the continental United States using Genesis Energy’s infrastructure. As such, this project will provide a bit of growth for Genesis Energy when it comes online either late this year or in early 2027. However, given that the Monument project is only expected to produce an average of 20,000 to 30,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day, this does not represent an enormous amount of growth for Genesis Energy. It is certainly not nearly as large as the Shenandoah project, which was already producing 100,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day by October of last year.

Shenandoah itself will also see its output increase over the next few years, which will result in additional incremental amounts of hydrocarbons flowing through Genesis Energy’s pipelines. Although even with Monument plus the additional phases of Shenandoah, it is not likely that forward growth will exceed what we saw in 2025.

Recent Performance Update

The last time that we discussed Genesis Energy was on December 10, 2025. As such, only about four months have passed since our last discussion, but it was certainly an eventful four months in the markets. We saw two, or perhaps three, major developments since the last time that we looked at this company.

The first major event came out of the technology sector, which saw a number of companies announce their capital spending plans for the coming years. The numbers were nothing short of enormous, with the technology industry collectively spending almost $1 trillion on data centers in 2025 and collectively planning to spend $4 trillion on these data centers by 2030. This spending represents a significant portion of their cash flows, and investors are questioning whether or not they will be able to monetize all of these capital expenditures effectively enough to earn an acceptable return on investment. As a result, technology company stocks have been getting sold off since the beginning of the year. The technology industry represents a sizable proportion of the S&P 500 Index (SP500) and most other American market capitalization-weighted indices, so the technology sell-off has been dragging down the indices since the start of the year.

Fortunately for investors in Genesis Energy, the company is not included in the S&P 500 Index (SPY), the NASDAQ 100 Index (QQQ), or the Russell 2000 Index (IWM). As such, it was not affected by the sell-off.

A second thing that occurred was the release of the February Producer Price Index. This index measures the change in prices over time that domestic producers receive for their output. For February 2026, this index showed an increase of 0.7% month-over-month. That is 8.4% annualized, and obviously, it is far above the Federal Reserve’s official 2% inflation target. While the Federal Reserve itself generally uses the Personal Consumption Expenditures Index as its measure of inflation, we should still watch the PPI since it is somewhat unlikely that businesses will absorb price increases of this magnitude without passing them on to their own customers. Thus, at some point, this will make its way down to consumer prices. When combined with the third major event that occurred since our last discussion, we can start to see why numerous market participants have begun to doubt whether or not the Federal Reserve will be able to reduce interest rates this year.

The final thing that had an impact on the markets since our last discussion on Genesis Energy was the outbreak of hostilities between the United States, Israel, and Iran. The military conflict began on February 28, 2026, and was quickly followed by Iran blocking traffic through the Strait of Hormuz and striking oil and gas production infrastructure throughout the Middle East. The blockade of the Strait of Hormuz alone has had a major impact on the energy industry due to the simple fact that approximately 20% to 25% of all oil consumed on a daily basis moves through that Strait. Thus, Iran’s actions have effectively reduced the supply of oil to the rest of the world and caused a noticeable increase in oil prices. The price of Brent crude oil has risen by 39.97% since the start of March:

Naturally, this has caused the price of gasoline and diesel fuel in the United States to rise significantly. Private transportation accounts for 14.832% of the Consumer Price Index, although the cost of gasoline is somewhat lower in terms of weighting in the index:

Thus, when combined with the surge in the Producer Price Index, investors have become much more worried about inflation and are doubting that the Federal Reserve will reduce interest rates this year. At the start of this year, stocks were priced as though rate cuts were a certainty, so this change in investors’ beliefs has triggered a sell-off in the market.

There is, however, one silver lining for us as energy investors. This is that energy stocks have surged significantly since the start of the year, and they have dramatically outperformed the S&P 500 Index:

As we can see, since the start of this year, the State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE) and the Alerian MLP Index (AMLP) have both risen while the S&P 500 Index has declined. It makes sense that the XLE would rise, since that index has a lot of exposure to upstream producers and the revenues, cash flows, and profits of upstream producers usually rise whenever crude oil prices rise. However, that is not the case for most midstream master limited partnerships. As we have discussed numerous times, midstream MLPs act much like toll roads and make their money based on resource volumes, not on resource values. The only way that they will truly benefit from rising oil prices is if today’s oil prices prove to be long-lasting enough to prompt an increase in production. Unless the war proves to be a long one and Iran is strong enough to keep the Strait of Hormuz largely blocked to traffic, that is not likely.

It is even less likely that a surge in energy prices will benefit Genesis Energy unless it proves to be a very long-lasting one. This is because of the fact that developing an offshore oil field is a very expensive and time-consuming process. The Shenandoah oil field was discovered in 2009, but it did not begin production until 2025. The Johan Sverdrup field in Norway was discovered in 2010, but it did not begin producing resources until 2019. While some offshore projects do not take a full decade to begin producing, the fact remains that developing an offshore oil field is a multi-year process at best. Oil and gas companies are not going to suddenly start developing an offshore field because of a geopolitical shock unless they expect that oil prices will remain high enough to justify the capital expenditures required for a very long time. The industry does not currently expect that the spike in prices from the Iran war will last long enough to start a production expansion program in the Gulf of America. Any increase in production by the industry in an attempt to take advantage of today’s prices will almost certainly be limited to America’s tight oil plays. Thus, Genesis Energy itself will probably not benefit in any noticeable way from the Iran war.

Despite the fact that Genesis Energy will likely not realize much benefit from the recent surge in crude oil prices, its common units have still risen by 14.66% since the last time that we discussed the company on December 10, 2025:

Perhaps interestingly, Genesis Energy did manage to outperform the Alerian MLP Index, and it outperformed the S&P 500 Index, but the latter was not a difficult accomplishment given that the index declined.

However, all of Genesis Energy’s gains since our last discussion came after the Iran War began. In fact, the company’s common unit price is down 2.55% since February 27, 2026. This strongly suggests that the company’s excellent price performance was driven by the strength of the most recent earnings report and the growth that the company experienced over the course of 2025. This is actually a rather nice thing to see. As Genesis Energy is unlikely to benefit from the recent surge in oil prices, if its equity rose because of it, then that would suggest that we should sell some of our units and take profits off of the table. However, the fact that the strong price performance was due to the cash flow growth that the company exhibited over the course of 2025 means that the company’s units are priced according to its current financial performance.

Distribution Analysis

As of the time of writing, Genesis Energy has a 4.09% distribution yield. This is lower than the yield of most other midstream master limited partnerships, as we can see in this chart:

Company Name

Current Distribution Yield

Genesis Energy

4.09%

Energy Transfer

7.08%

Enterprise Products Partners

5.86%

MPLX

7.70%

Plains All American Pipeline

7.54%

Western Midstream Partners

8.84%

As shown, Genesis Energy’s distribution yield is 177 basis points lower than that of Enterprise Products Partners, which is the only other master limited partnership shown that has a yield of less than 7%. This is not exactly ideal, since a lower yield means that the company will provide us with less income than if we sold and purchased another company’s common units.

However, given that Genesis Energy recently went through a growth spurt, it is possible that distribution growth could be on the horizon (the company did already increase its distribution for the most recent quarter, although it was only a slight increase). Let us take a look at its finances to see how much room it has to do that.

For the full-year period that ended on December 31, 2025, Genesis Energy reported available cash before reserves of $149.121 million. This is a non-GAAP figure that theoretically tells us the amount of cash that was generated by the company’s ordinary operations and is available to be distributed to the common unitholders. The company currently has 122.5 million common units outstanding, so the recently raised distribution of $0.18 per common share per quarter would cost the company $88.2 million. This puts the company’s coverage ratio at 1.69x, which is well above the 1.20x ratio that Wall Street analysts usually find to be sustainable.

However, we should note that some of the biggest drivers of cash flow growth for Genesis Energy over the course of 2025 did not provide revenue to the company for the entire 2025 fiscal year. These drivers will be contributing revenue to Genesis Energy over the entire 2026 fiscal year. As such, the company’s cash available for distribution should come in higher in 2026 than the 2025 figure. Thus, Genesis Energy’s coverage ratio should improve somewhat, and this may set the company up for another distribution increase in early 2027.

Quant System Alert

One thing that is important to mention about Genesis Energy is that Seeking Alpha’s Quant Valuation System has rated the common units as a “Strong Sell” and warns that the equity may end up performing badly going forward:

There are two reasons given for this warning. The first is that, unlike most other energy companies, Genesis Energy has not had any analysts increase their earnings predictions over the past ninety days:

I agree with this assessment. As I mentioned earlier in this article, the recent surge in energy prices will likely benefit upstream oil and gas producers, but it is unlikely to have much of an impact on most midstream companies. Genesis Energy itself is highly unlikely to be positively affected due to the fact that offshore oil and gas production cannot be quickly increased, and upstream energy companies are highly unlikely to start new projects in the Gulf of America due to a temporary increase in the price of crude oil. Thus, there is no reason for analysts to increase their earnings estimates for Genesis Energy because the Iran War-driven increase in oil prices has not changed the fundamentals at all.

The second reason for the poor Quant rating is that Genesis Energy’s net income margin is very low relative to the rest of the energy sector:

Please note that this comparison is against all energy companies, not just against midstream companies. This is important because of the fact that midstream companies tend to have incredibly high depreciation and amortization due to the high capital expenditures required to construct pipelines, terminals, and other infrastructure. This is why we typically look at their cash flow or EBITDA rather than net income. As such, the weak net income margin and net income per employee ratios might not be very important for this company.

With that said, Genesis Energy does still have a relatively high debt load, especially when compared to other midstream partnerships. As of December 31, 2025, Genesis Energy’s debt-to-TTM adjusted EBITDA ratio is 5.12x. That is far above the sub-4.0x ratios that most midstream master limited partnerships possess. As regular readers are likely well aware, this high level of debt has been one of my biggest concerns about this company since we first started discussing it back in 2018. Personally, I would like to see the company focus on paying this debt down somewhat before hiking the distribution significantly, but it is unclear what the company will actually do.

Conclusion

In conclusion, Genesis Energy is a unique master limited partnership that could add some diversity to a portfolio that contains onshore pipeline partnerships. The company has just gone through a transformative year that saw its cash flow increase significantly. That is nice, but unfortunately, it probably will not be able to continue to deliver growth at nearly the same pace going forward. The company’s distribution yield is low relative to its peers, and its debt remains high.

At this point, it might make sense to take some gains off of the table. While I would not sell out of any position that you may have in Genesis Energy completely, it could be a good idea to take some of the gains, given that better income can be obtained elsewhere.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
